items.title: Rethinking classroom design to improve learning
items.author: Júlio César Claudino dos Santos
items.content: Rethinking classroom design to improve learning

items.title: A large-scale comparison of divergent creativity in humans and large language models
items.author: Brian Uzzi
items.content: Wang et al. compare human and large language model (LLM) creativity via Divergent Association Task, finding that humans perform slightly better on average. Efforts to enhance LLM performance, such as adopting genius personas or using strategic prompts, show mixed results.

items.title: Homophobia, economic precarity and the well-being of sexual and gender diverse people in a 153-country survey
items.author: Bruno Ventelou
items.content: A cross-country survey among 82,324 sexual and gender diverse people finds that family rejection, homophobic reactions, and institutional stigma and discrimination are linked with individual well-being, especially for those facing poverty.

items.title: Indigenization and inclusion in Chinese academia
items.author: Hong Liu
items.content: Huang, Cao and Liu compile a dataset of 3,534 profiles of members of China’s national academies to trace academic exchanges over 120 years and find evidence for a decline in foreign-educated academics.

items.title: Shared sensitivity to data distribution during learning in humans and transformer networks
items.author: Christopher Summerfield
items.content: Pesnot Lerousseau and Summerfield compare humans to transformer neural networks in a learning task designed to distinguish ‘in-weights’ and ‘in-context’ learning processes.

items.title: Large-scale mega-analysis indicates that serial dependence deteriorates perceptual decision-making
items.author: David Pascucci
items.content: Drawing on the largest dataset of its kind, this Article shows that, rather than improving performance, serial dependence makes perceptual decisions more uncertain. These results challenge the prevailing view and call for a rethinking of the role of serial dependence in human behaviour.

items.title: Higher-order interactions shape collective human behaviour
items.author: Matjaž Perc
items.content: Battiston et al. discuss the emerging paradigm of higher-order network science and its applications to social systems and human dynamics.

items.title: Implications of Australia’s under-16 social media ban
items.author: Shin Ling Wu
items.content: Implications of Australia’s under-16 social media ban
